symbols.name: \(\widetilde P\)
symbols.def: Full-data extension whose observed pushforward is \(P\).

symbols.name: \(H_1\)
symbols.def: State immediately before the first decision.

symbols.name: \(H_2\)
symbols.def: State immediately before the second decision.

symbols.name: \(r\)
symbols.def: Index of a generic Hölder ball.

symbols.name: \(\mathcal G_d^t(M)\)
symbols.def: Transition densities Hölder in the incoming state under \(L^1(dh_2)\).

symbols.name: \(\widehat m_1\)
symbols.def: Foldwise direct wavelet-sieve regression of \(\widehat Z_2\) on \(H_1\) among \(A_1=1\).

symbols.name: \(\mathbb P_n\)
symbols.def: Average over evaluation-fold observations, aggregated over folds.

symbols.name: \(\phi_\ell\)
symbols.def: Boundary-corrected orthonormal wavelet indexed by \(\ell\in\mathbb N\).

symbols.name: \(k_{\pi1},k_{\pi2},k_2,k_1\)
symbols.def: Dimensions for first propensity, second propensity, terminal regression, and direct backward regression fits.

symbols.name: \(q_1,q_2\)
symbols.def: Orders of first- and second-stage degenerate U-statistic corrections.

symbols.name: \(\mathcal R_{12}\)
symbols.def: First-stage product remainder when \(\widehat m_1\) is fitted to \(\widehat Z_2\).

symbols.name: \(\mathfrak R_n(\mathcal M)\)
symbols.def: Worst-case root risk optimized over sample-measurable estimators.

novel.justification: A two-decision physiologic-challenge protocol constructs the second state deterministically from the complete prespecified panel of biomarkers, dosing history, and device telemetry. The terminal assay protocol uses that state only, and the state-building code is retained with the data.

The second randomizer reads only the versioned second-state record. The randomization log records the supplied fields, making this restriction auditable in a protocol whose decision rule excludes earlier history.

A calibrated state-response map with additive noise whose density has integrable derivatives through order \(t\) yields an \(L^1\)-Hölder conditional transition law. This is a substantive property of continuously measured physiologic states, not a requirement to estimate the full conditional density.

assumptions.condition: \(g(h_2\mid h_1)=g(h_2\mid h_1')\quad\text{for Lebesgue-almost every }h_2\text{ and every }h_1,h_1'\in[0,1]^d\).

definitions.name: \(\mathcal M(\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\beta,t;M,c)\)
definitions.construction: \(\{P:\exists\widetilde P\text{ such that }(P,\widetilde P)\text{ satisfies ass:observed-pushforward, ass:consistency, ass:exchangeability-one, ass:exchangeability-two, ass:strict-overlap, ass:state-sufficiency-outcome, ass:state-sufficiency-treatment, ass:holder-pi-one, ass:holder-pi-two, ass:holder-terminal, and ass:transition-holder}\}\).

definitions.name: direct sequential-HOIF handle
definitions.construction: On each training fold, take \(V_k=\operatorname{span}\{\phi_1,\ldots,\phi_k\}\), fit \(\widehat\pi_1\) and \(\widehat\pi_2\) by squared-loss sieve regressions in \(V_{k_{\pi1}}\) and \(V_{k_{\pi2}}\), and clip them to \([c/2,1-c/2]\). Fit \(\widehat m_2\) by squared-loss sieve regression of \(Y\) on \(H_2\) among \(A_1=A_2=1\) in \(V_{k_2}\). Set \(\widehat Z_2=\widehat m_2(H_2)+A_2\{Y-\widehat m_2(H_2)\}/\widehat\pi_2(H_2)\), and fit \(\widehat m_1\) by squared-loss sieve regression of \(\widehat Z_2\) on \(H_1\) among \(A_1=1\) in \(V_{k_1}\). On independent evaluation folds, begin with \(\mathbb P_n[\widehat m_1(H_1)+A_1\{\widehat Z_2-\widehat m_1(H_1)\}/\widehat\pi_1(H_1)]\); use \(K_k(x,x')=\sum_{\ell=1}^k\phi_\ell(x)\phi_\ell(x')\) to derive first- and second-stage degenerate U-statistic corrections of orders \((q_1,q_2)\) for the projected residual products.

definitions.name: \(\mathcal R_{12}\)
definitions.construction: \(\mathcal R_{12}(\widehat\eta)=E_P[\{\pi_1(H_1)-\widehat\pi_1(H_1)\}\{\widehat m_1(H_1)-m_1(H_1)\}/\widehat\pi_1(H_1)]\), where \(\widehat m_1\) is fitted to \(\widehat Z_2\).

definitions.name: chained fuzzy-mixture handle
definitions.construction: Use disjoint wavelet blocks at two resolutions. Pair localized signs in \(\pi_1\) with signs in a normalized treated transition density \(g(\cdot\mid h_1)\), pair the latter with localized signs in \(m_2\), and compare two convex mixtures indexed by opposite products of the three signs. Calibrate block widths and amplitudes against the Hölder radii, strict overlap, target separation, and mixture Hellinger distance.

statements.justification: It identifies the causal mean as an observed-data functional on the stated class.
statements.gap: Luedtke et al. give backward-regression identification but do not formulate the transition-\(L^1\)-Hölder minimax class.
statements.consumer: It supplies the functional for the Cinelli et al. time-varying optimality problem and for the two-decision physiologic-challenge regime.

statements.justification: It makes incoming-state transition regularity the primitive regularity of the first composed regression.
statements.gap: Single-time HOIF formulations have no transition-mediated backward regression.
statements.consumer: It identifies the approximation channel used in the two-decision physiologic-challenge regime.

statements.statement: Is \(\mathfrak R_n(\mathcal M)\asymp n^{-1/2}+n^{-r_1}+n^{-r_2}\), where \(r_1=2(\alpha_1+t)/(2(\alpha_1+t)+d)\) and \(r_2=2(\alpha_2+\beta)/(2(\alpha_2+\beta)+d)\)? In particular, is \(\mathfrak R_n(\mathcal M)\asymp n^{-1/2}\) if and only if \(\min\{\alpha_1+t,\alpha_2+\beta\}\ge d/2\)?
statements.justification: This isolates whether the transition law, rather than terminal-outcome smoothness, governs the first longitudinal nonparametric channel.
statements.gap: Cinelli et al. (2025, Section 3.5.3) state that non-root-\(n\) optimality for time-varying effects is open. Bonvini, Kennedy, and Keele (2025, EcoSta Abstract A0576) report two-time-point HOIF rates for anisotropic and additive structured models; their published abstract does not state a frontier for the isotropic Markov class whose sole transition regularity is incoming-state \(L^1\)-Hölder smoothness. This question therefore does not claim to dominate their structured classes.
statements.consumer: It directly resolves the time-varying non-root-\(n\) optimality question identified by Cinelli et al. for the two-decision physiologic-challenge regime.

statements.statement: Can the direct sequential-HOIF handle yield foldwise choices of \((k_{\pi1},k_{\pi2},k_2,k_1,q_1,q_2)\) and explicit degenerate U-statistic kernels such that its estimator has RMSE \(O(n^{-1/2}+n^{-r_1}+n^{-r_2})\) uniformly over \(\mathcal M\), while its correction cancels \(\mathcal R_{12}(\widehat\eta)\) to smaller order? Is a coordinatewise-minimal pair \((q_1,q_2)\) sufficient, or is a genuinely multi-index correction required?
statements.justification: The direct pseudo-outcome regression avoids estimating a transition density whose stated class supplies no outgoing-state regularity.
statements.gap: Luedtke et al. give first-order sequential remainder identities, while Bonvini, Kennedy, and Keele report higher-order two-time-point estimators in anisotropic and additive settings; neither published description specifies the correction-order frontier for this transition-\(L^1\)-Hölder Markov class.
statements.consumer: It determines whether the two-decision physiologic-challenge regime needs a correction architecture beyond the two-time-point HOIF settings reported by Bonvini, Kennedy, and Keele.

statements.statement: Can the chained fuzzy-mixture handle produce two pairs of mixtures in \(\mathcal M\), one at the \(r_1\) scale and one at the \(r_2\) scale, whose product-law Hellinger distances are bounded and whose \(\psi\)-separations are respectively of orders \(n^{-r_1}\) and \(n^{-r_2}\)?
statements.justification: A legal longitudinal converse must generate the first backward regression through the transition law instead of treating it as variation independent.
statements.gap: Robins, Tchetgen Tchetgen, Li, and van der Vaart (2009, Section 2, Theorem 2.1) use convex mixtures for single-time multiparameter functionals; the unresolved issue is a normalized transition-mediated sign construction for the longitudinal g-formula.
statements.consumer: It provides the minimax lower-bound evidence needed to answer the Cinelli et al. time-varying optimality question for a two-decision physiologic-challenge regime.

statements.statement: On \(\mathcal M_{\mathrm{ind}}\), \(m_1\) is constant and \(\mathfrak R_n(\mathcal M_{\mathrm{ind}})\asymp n^{-1/2}+n^{-r_2}\).
statements.justification: The restriction removes the transition-mediated first-stage channel.
statements.gap: It is the known single-time missing-response benchmark, not a novelty claim.
statements.consumer: It provides a reduction check against the single-time HOIF benchmark of Robins et al. (2017).

sampling_model.design: Independent observational trajectories from an observed law admitting the stated full-data extension.
sampling_model.treatment: Two binary decisions under sequential exchangeability and strict overlap.
sampling_model.nuisance_estimation: Cross-fitted clipped wavelet-sieve regressions for \(\pi_1,\pi_2,m_2\), followed by direct regression of the augmented second-stage pseudo-outcome for \(m_1\); no transition-density estimator is used.

tldr: The proposal studies the two-period Markov g-formula when the first backward regression is an integral against a transition law. Its rate question asks whether the two minimax channels are indexed by \(\alpha_1+t\) and \(\alpha_2+\beta\). The estimator question starts from a fully specified direct pseudo-outcome regression and wavelet-kernel correction handle, thereby avoiding unsupported estimation of the full transition density, while the converse question uses a transition-mediated fuzzy-mixture route.

project_justification.gap: Time-varying causal effects lack a settled non-root-\(n\) minimax theory. First-order longitudinal work gives sequential robustness, and single-time HOIF work gives sharp slow-rate analyses, while Bonvini, Kennedy, and Keele report distinct anisotropic and additive two-time-point constructions.
project_justification.niche: The isotropic Markov class makes incoming-state \(L^1\)-Hölder transition regularity and the recursively fitted pseudo-outcome error explicit without imposing outgoing-state smoothness solely to estimate a full transition density.
project_justification.fill: The project determines or refutes the stated rate, derives a direct-regression sequential correction architecture, and constructs a normalized transition-mediated lower-bound mixture.

related_work: Cinelli, Feller, Imbens, Kennedy, Magliacane, and Zubizarreta (2025, Section 3.5.3) state that non-root-\(n\) optimality for time-varying treatment effects remains open. Luedtke, Sofrygin, van der Laan, and Carone derive backward-regression and sequential-double-robustness identities, but not low-regularity minimax rates. Robins, Li, Tchetgen Tchetgen, and van der Vaart develop higher-order influence functions and sharp slow-rate calculations for single-time nonlinear functionals. Bonvini, Kennedy, and Keele (2025, EcoSta Abstract A0576) consider the two-time-point g-formula with HOIF estimators in anisotropic smoothness and additive structured models and establish minimax optimality for some settings. The present transition-\(L^1\)-Hölder Markov class is informed by, but does not claim to extend, that work: the public abstract does not specify this class or the proposed \(\alpha_1+t\) frontier. Robins, Tchetgen Tchetgen, Li, and van der Vaart (2009, Section 2, Theorem 2.1) provide the two-convex-mixture device whose sign pairing must here be routed through a normalized transition law.
technical_internal_limitation: The core concerns exact two-period state sufficiency. Protocols whose second randomizer or terminal assay uses omitted history can introduce an additional first-stage roughness channel.
honest_scope: The stated question fixes smoothness indices, overlap, and a two-period horizon. Adaptation, approximate state sufficiency, and longer horizons remain open. The state-independent-transition proposition is a single-time reduction check.

bibliography.citation: C. Cinelli, A. Feller, G. Imbens, E. Kennedy, S. Magliacane, and J. Zubizarreta (2025). \"Challenges in Statistics: A Dozen Challenges in Causality and Causal Inference.\" \emph{arXiv:2508.17099}.
M. Bonvini, E. Kennedy, and L. Keele (2025). \"Higher-order Estimators of Time-varying Effects in Anisotropic Smoothness Models.\" EcoSta 2025, Abstract A0576.
A. Luedtke, O. Sofrygin, M. van der Laan, and M. Carone (2017). \"Sequential Double Robustness in Right-Censored Longitudinal Models.\" \emph{arXiv:1705.02459}.
J. Robins, L. Li, E. Tchetgen Tchetgen, and A. van der Vaart (2008). \"Higher Order Influence Functions and Minimax Estimation of Nonlinear Functionals.\" \emph{IMS Collections}.
J. Robins, E. Tchetgen Tchetgen, L. Li, and A. van der Vaart (2009). \"Semiparametric Minimax Rates.\" \emph{Electronic Journal of Statistics} 3:1305--1321.
J. Robins, L. Li, R. Mukherjee, E. Tchetgen Tchetgen, and A. van der Vaart (2017). \"Higher Order Estimating Equations for High-Dimensional Models.\" \emph{Annals of Statistics} 45(5):1951--1987.
M. Hernán and J. Robins (2020). \emph{Causal Inference: What If}. Chapman and Hall/CRC.
A. Tsybakov (2009). \emph{Introduction to Nonparametric Estimation}. Springer.
O. Kallenberg (2002). \emph{Foundations of Modern Probability}. Springer.

comparator_promise_table.comparator_claim: Optimality for time-varying treatment effects outside the square-root-\(n\) regime is entirely open.
Two-time-point g-formula HOIF estimators have calculated bias and variance in anisotropic and additive structured models, with minimax-optimal rates for some settings.
Sequential double robustness follows from a first-order longitudinal remainder analysis.
Higher-order influence functions attain sharp slow rates for single-time nonlinear functionals.
Projection higher-order estimating equations provide single-time slow-rate benchmarks.
Two convex mixtures yield lower bounds for multiparameter single-time semiparametric functionals.
